three_phase_commit.py - reference impl. All numbers below feed THREE_PHASE_COMMIT.md.
python stdlib only.

========================================================================
  SECTION A: the three phases  (CanCommit? -> PreCommit -> DoCommit)
========================================================================
N = 3 participants, all vote YES (a clean commit).

  Coordinator -> P0: CanCommit?
  P0 -> Coordinator: YES  (P now READY)
  Coordinator -> P1: CanCommit?
  P1 -> Coordinator: YES  (P now READY)
  Coordinator -> P2: CanCommit?
  P2 -> Coordinator: YES  (P now READY)
  Phase 1 result: 3/3 YES -> UNANIMOUS, proceed to PreCommit
  Coordinator -> P0: PreCommit
  P0 -> Coordinator: ACK  (P now PRECOMMITTED)
  Coordinator -> P1: PreCommit
  P1 -> Coordinator: ACK  (P now PRECOMMITTED)
  Coordinator -> P2: PreCommit
  P2 -> Coordinator: ACK  (P now PRECOMMITTED)
  Phase 2 result: all PRECOMMITTED, proceed to DoCommit
  Coordinator -> P0: DoCommit
  P0 -> Coordinator: ACK  (P now COMMITTED)
  Coordinator -> P1: DoCommit
  P1 -> Coordinator: ACK  (P now COMMITTED)
  Coordinator -> P2: DoCommit
  P2 -> Coordinator: ACK  (P now COMMITTED)
  Phase 3 result: all COMMITTED -> transaction committed

Final states: P0=COMMITTED, P1=COMMITTED, P2=COMMITTED

Read it as six vertical sweeps (3 phases x request+reply):
  Phase 1: CanCommit down  -> YES up     (everyone -> READY)
  Phase 2: PreCommit down  -> ACK up     (everyone -> PRECOMMITTED)
  Phase 3: DoCommit down   -> ACK up     (everyone -> COMMITTED)

Cost vs 2PC: 3PC adds the PreCommit round trip -> 3 round trips
(2PC needs only 2). That extra round trip is the PRICE of
non-blocking recovery (Sections B and C). With N=3 participants:
  3PC messages = 6*N = 18   (CanCommit+vote, PreCommit+ACK, DoCommit+ACK)
  3PC round trips = 3
  2PC messages   = 4*N = 12   round trips = 2

[check] all participants COMMITTED and agree?  OK

========================================================================
  SECTION B: coordinator crash AFTER PreCommit  -> participants commit autonomously
========================================================================
N = 3 participants, all vote YES. Coordinator crashes AFTER
delivering PreCommit but BEFORE DoCommit.

  Coordinator -> P0: CanCommit?
  P0 -> Coordinator: YES  (P now READY)
  Coordinator -> P1: CanCommit?
  P1 -> Coordinator: YES  (P now READY)
  Coordinator -> P2: CanCommit?
  P2 -> Coordinator: YES  (P now READY)
  Phase 1 result: 3/3 YES -> UNANIMOUS, proceed to PreCommit
  Coordinator -> P0: PreCommit
  P0 -> Coordinator: ACK  (P now PRECOMMITTED)
  Coordinator -> P1: PreCommit
  P1 -> Coordinator: ACK  (P now PRECOMMITTED)
  Coordinator -> P2: PreCommit
  P2 -> Coordinator: ACK  (P now PRECOMMITTED)
  Phase 2 result: all PRECOMMITTED, proceed to DoCommit
  *** COORDINATOR CRASHES after PreCommit (before sending DoCommit) ***

States at crash: P0=PRECOMMITTED, P1=PRECOMMITTED, P2=PRECOMMITTED
Every participant is PRECOMMITTED. The PreCommit message is a
CERTIFICATE that the coordinator received YES from ALL participants
(it only sends PreCommit after a unanimous YES). So the commit is
already decided -- DoCommit was just the announcement. The
participants run the TERMINATION PROTOCOL and finish on their own:

  decision = COMMIT
  reason   = ALL PRECOMMITTED -> everyone voted YES -> commit safe

Final states: P0=COMMITTED, P1=COMMITTED, P2=COMMITTED

This is the win 3PC buys over 2PC: the coordinator died, yet NO
participant blocks -- they all reach COMMITTED without waiting for
recovery. In 2PC this exact crash leaves everyone stuck (Section D).

[check] non-blocking: all participants reached COMMITTED without the coordinator?  OK

========================================================================
  SECTION C: coordinator crash BEFORE PreCommit  -> participants abort safely
========================================================================
N = 3 participants, all vote YES. Coordinator crashes AFTER
Phase 1 but BEFORE sending PreCommit.

  Coordinator -> P0: CanCommit?
  P0 -> Coordinator: YES  (P now READY)
  Coordinator -> P1: CanCommit?
  P1 -> Coordinator: YES  (P now READY)
  Coordinator -> P2: CanCommit?
  P2 -> Coordinator: YES  (P now READY)
  Phase 1 result: 3/3 YES -> UNANIMOUS, proceed to PreCommit
  *** COORDINATOR CRASHES after Phase 1 (before sending PreCommit) ***

States at crash: P0=READY, P1=READY, P2=READY
Every participant is READY (voted YES) but NONE received PreCommit.
A READY participant CANNOT assume the commit will happen: maybe the
coordinator crashed, or maybe it crashed BECAUSE some OTHER
participant voted NO and the coordinator was about to abort. A
READY participant has no way to tell. So the safe move is to ABORT.
The termination protocol:

  decision = ABORT
  reason   = some participant still READY (uncertain) -> cannot guarantee a unanimous YES -> abort to be safe

Final states: P0=ABORTED, P1=ABORTED, P2=ABORTED

Note this is STILL non-blocking (nobody waits for the coordinator)
-- they actively decide ABORT. It is just a conservative decision.
Compare with 2PC: a READY participant after this same crash would
BLOCK, because it cannot safely abort OR commit. 3PC's PreCommit
phase is what splits 'uncertain' (READY) from 'safe to commit'
(PRECOMMITTED), turning a block into a decision.

[check] non-blocking: all participants reached a single final state (ABORTED)?  OK

========================================================================
  SECTION D: 2PC vs 3PC  (round trips, messages, and the blocking window)
========================================================================
Same transaction, N participants, clean commit (all vote YES).

| protocol | phases | round trips | messages = sweeps*N | coordinator crash while READY |
|----------|--------|-------------|---------------------|-----------------------------|
| 2PC      | 2      | 2           | 4*N msgs       | BLOCKS (participant cannot decide) |
| 3PC      | 3      | 3           | 6*N msgs       | NON-BLOCKING (PreCommit settles it) |

DEMO (N=3, all vote YES): coordinator crashes right after Phase 1.
  2PC path:
    states after crash: P0=READY, P1=READY, P2=READY
    can a participant decide COMMIT or ABORT on its own? NO -> BLOCK
    (it does not know if a peer voted NO, or if the coordinator
     already decided commit. It holds its locks and WAITS.)
  3PC path:
    states after crash: P0=READY, P1=READY, P2=READY
    termination decision (without coordinator): ABORT
    final states: P0=ABORTED, P1=ABORTED, P2=ABORTED
    blocked? NO -- participants actively decided ABORT.

Scaling messages & round trips with N participants (commit path):
| N participants | 2PC msgs=4N | 2PC RT | 3PC msgs=6N | 3PC RT | 3PC latency overhead |
|---------------|-------------|--------|-------------|--------|----------------------|
| 1             | 4           | 2      | 6           | 3      | +2 msgs, +1 RT        |
| 2             | 8           | 2      | 12          | 3      | +4 msgs, +1 RT        |
| 3             | 12          | 2      | 18          | 3      | +6 msgs, +1 RT        |
| 5             | 20          | 2      | 30          | 3      | +10 msgs, +1 RT        |
| 10            | 40          | 2      | 60          | 3      | +20 msgs, +1 RT        |

The trade in one line: 3PC pays 1 extra round trip (+50% messages)
to convert 2PC's blocking crash window into a participant-decided one.
Whether that is worth it depends on how often the coordinator crashes
vs how much you hate the extra latency -- and, crucially, whether your
network is synchronous enough for the assumption to hold (Section E).

[check] 2PC blocks after phase-1 crash and 3PC does not?  OK

========================================================================
  SECTION E: why 3PC isn't used in practice  (synchrony assumption + FLP)
========================================================================
3PC's termination protocol (Section B/C) rests on one assumption:
a participant can RELIABLY tell 'the coordinator crashed' from 'the
coordinator is slow'. That requires a SYNCHRONOUS network -- bounded
message delay AND bounded processor speed, so a timeout is PROOF of
death. Real networks are ASYNCHRONOUS: a message can be arbitrarily
delayed, and a 'slow' coordinator may simply be stuck behind GC or a
network hiccup.

The formal wall is the FLP impossibility result:
  Fischer, Lynch, Paterson (1985), 'Impossibility of Distributed
  Consensus with One Faulty Process', JACM.
  -> In a truly asynchronous network, NO protocol can guarantee both
     safety AND liveness if even ONE process may crash.
3PC gets non-blocking liveness by ASSUMING synchrony; the moment you
drop that assumption (i.e. the real world), a timeout can fire while
the coordinator is merely delayed. Then two groups of participants
can run the termination protocol on stale/contradictory views and
SPLIT BRAIN: one group commits, the other aborts. Safety is gone.

On top of that, 3PC costs 3 round trips vs 2PC's 2 -- 50% more latency
on the happy path, for a crash that rarely happens. So even setting
FLP aside, the price/benefit is poor for most workloads.

What real systems use instead:
| approach | idea | why it beats 3PC | used by |
|----------|------|------------------|---------|
| Paxos Commit (Gray & Lamport 2005) |
|   idea: run consensus (Paxos/Raft) to agree the commit decision |
|   edge: tolerates partial synchrony + leader failover; no split brain |
|   used: Spanner, CockroachDB (Raft), FoundationDB |
| Saga (Garcia-Molina & Salem 1987) |
|   idea: sequence of local sub-transactions with compensations |
|   edge: NO distributed commit at all; avoids 2PC/3PC entirely |
|   used: microservices, event-driven workflows |
| Coordinator recovery + timeouts (2PC) |
|   idea: accept blocking, engineer fast coordinator recovery |
|   edge: simple; blocking window is rare and short with HA coord |
|   used: XA transactions in many RDBMS / message brokers |

Bottom line: 3PC is the canonical proof that 'add a phase, remove the
blocking window' is possible IN PRINCIPLE. FLP is the proof it is NOT
possible in practice. The lesson the field took: don't build commit on
timeouts alone -- build it on consensus (Paxos/Raft), which is what
every modern transactional system does. 🔗 See PAXOS.md / RAFT.md.

[check] 3PC is non-blocking under synchrony, unsafe under FLP:  OK

========================================================================
  GOLD CHECK  (safety + non-blocking: every crash -> one uniform final decision)
========================================================================
Canonical setup: N = 3 participants, deterministic votes.

(1) crash AFTER  PreCommit: decision=COMMIT, agree=True, states=P0=COMMITTED, P1=COMMITTED, P2=COMMITTED
(2) crash BEFORE PreCommit: decision=ABORT, agree=True, states=P0=ABORTED, P1=ABORTED, P2=ABORTED
(3) crash mid-DoCommit (P0 COMMITTED, rest PRECOMMITTED): decision=COMMIT, agree=True, states=P0=COMMITTED, P1=COMMITTED, P2=COMMITTED
(4) P1 votes NO: decision=ABORT, agree=True, states=P0=ABORTED, P1=ABORTED, P2=ABORTED
(5) crash before PreCommit (all READY): decision=ABORT, agree=True, states=P0=ABORTED, P1=ABORTED, P2=ABORTED

SAFETY invariant (every crash -> single uniform final state): True
NON-BLOCKING (termination always returns a decision, never waits): True

GOLD scalars (pinned for three_phase_commit.html):
  phases_2pc                         = 2
  phases_3pc                         = 3
  round_trips_2pc                    = 2
  round_trips_3pc                    = 3
  messages_2pc(N=3) = 4*N          = 12
  messages_3pc(N=3) = 6*N          = 18
  crash_after_precommit -> decision  = COMMIT
  crash_before_precommit -> decision = ABORT
  crash_mid_docommit -> decision     = COMMIT
  all_crash_cases_uniform            = True
  never_blocks                       = True

[check] all gold identities reproduce from the protocol:  OK

========================================================================
  DONE - all sections printed
========================================================================
